title: Quiz: Coding Theorems
description: Test your understanding of coding theory and error-correcting codes

questions.question: A [7, 4, 3] linear code can correct up to how many errors?
questions.explanation: A code with minimum distance d can correct t = floor((d-1)/2) errors. For d=3, t = floor(2/2) = 1 error.

questions.question: The rate of an [n, k, d] linear code is:
questions.explanation: The rate R = k/n represents the fraction of bits that carry information. The remaining (n-k) bits are redundancy for error correction.

questions.question: A perfect code is one that:

options.text: Meets the Hamming bound with equality

questions.explanation: A perfect code exactly meets the Hamming (sphere-packing) bound. The Hamming spheres around codewords perfectly tile the space with no overlaps or gaps.

questions.question: The Gilbert-Varshamov bound proves that good codes exist but doesn't provide an explicit construction.
questions.explanation: True. The GV bound is an existence result showing that codes with rate R ≥ 1 - H(δ) exist for relative distance δ. It uses a greedy or random argument without explicit construction.

questions.question: Which codes were the first to be proven capacity-achieving with polynomial complexity?
questions.explanation: Polar codes, invented by Arikan in 2009, were the first codes proven to achieve capacity with O(n log n) encoding and decoding complexity.

questions.question: The syndrome s = Hr^T of a received word r tells us:

options.text: The minimum distance of the code

questions.explanation: The syndrome depends only on the error pattern, not the codeword. If s = 0, either no error occurred or an undetectable error. The syndrome helps identify and correct errors.

questions.question: LDPC codes use dense parity-check matrices for efficient decoding.
questions.explanation: False. LDPC stands for Low-Density Parity-Check. The sparse structure enables efficient iterative (belief propagation) decoding with complexity O(n) per iteration.

questions.question: Which are examples of perfect binary codes? (Select all)
questions.explanation: Perfect binary codes: Hamming codes [2^r-1, 2^r-1-r, 3], the Golay code [23,12,7], and repetition codes [n,1,n] for odd n. Reed-Solomon codes are not binary.
